coredata.dc:title: A novel ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor based on graphene quantum dots/Cu-MOF nanocomposite for the on-site determination of Staphylococcus aureus

coredata.dc:description:
The sensitive detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) holds great practical importance for ensuring public health and food safety. In this study, a sensitivity and stability ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor using graphene quantum dots/[Cu2.5 (benzotriazole-5-COO)1.5 (benzotriazole-5-COOH)0.5 (μ-Cl)0.5 (μ3-OH)-(H2O)]·3 H2O nanocomposite (GQDs/Cu-MOF) was constructed for S. aureus detection. The GQDs enhanced the sensitivity of the electrochemical aptasensor due to their excellent conductivity and provided stability through their abundant carboxyl groups. The Cu-MOF, possessing electrical activity, not only enhanced the performance of the electrochemical aptasensor but also served as a signal label. The single-stranded DNA1 (S1) was immobilized on the surface of a GQDs/Cu-MOF/screen-printed carbon electrode (S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE) as the sensing interface. Subsequently, the S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE was hybridized with the probe DNA-ferrocene (S2-Fc), resulting in the generation of electrochemical signals from Cu-MOF (I
Cu-MOF ) and S2-ferrocene (I
S2-FC ) within the system. However, the electron transfer performance of DNA at the sensing interface was compromised, leading to a reduction in the I
Cu-MOF . When S. aureus was present in the system, S2-Fc reacted with it and detached from the sensing surface, resulting in a gradual decrease in I
S2-FC and an increase in I
Cu-MOF . Then a ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor was established for S. aureus detection with remarkable sensitivity (0.97 CFU·mL−1), excellent stability, and a broad linear range. Furthermore, the aptasensor was successfully applied to detect S. aureus in tap water, milk, Lonicera japonica, urine, and Zhangjiang River. Additionally, this aptasensor design can be adapted for the detection of other foodborne pathogens, which indicates that the design scheme of the aptasensor has good universality.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The sensitive detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) holds great practical importance for ensuring public health and food safety. In this study, a sensitivity and stability ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor using graphene quantum dots/[Cu2.5 (benzotriazole-5-COO)1.5 (benzotriazole-5-COOH)0.5 (μ-Cl)0.5 (μ3-OH)-(H2O)]·3 H2O nanocomposite (GQDs/Cu-MOF) was constructed for S. aureus detection. The GQDs enhanced the sensitivity of the electrochemical aptasensor due to their excellent conductivity and provided stability through their abundant carboxyl groups. The Cu-MOF, possessing electrical activity, not only enhanced the performance of the electrochemical aptasensor but also served as a signal label. The single-stranded DNA1 (S1) was immobilized on the surface of a GQDs/Cu-MOF/screen-printed carbon electrode (S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE) as the sensing interface. Subsequently, the S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE was hybridized with the probe DNA-ferrocene (S2-Fc), resulting in the generation of electrochemical signals from Cu-MOF (I
Cu-MOF ) and S2-ferrocene (I
S2-FC ) within the system. However, the electron transfer performance of DNA at the sensing interface was compromised, leading to a reduction in the I
Cu-MOF . When S. aureus was present in the system, S2-Fc reacted with it and detached from the sensing surface, resulting in a gradual decrease in I
S2-FC and an increase in I
Cu-MOF . Then a ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor was established for S. aureus detection with remarkable sensitivity (0.97 CFU·mL−1), excellent stability, and a broad linear range. Furthermore, the aptasensor was successfully applied to detect S. aureus in tap water, milk, Lonicera japonica, urine, and Zhangjiang River. Additionally, this aptasensor design can be adapted for the detection of other foodborne pathogens, which indicates that the design scheme of the aptasensor has good universality.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A novel ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor based on GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposite for the on-site determination of Staphylococcus aureus in tap water, milk, Lonicera japonica urine, and Zhangjiang River.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, foodborne pathogen-related public health issues have garnered global attention. Effective foodborne pathogen detection is crucial for ensuring food safety, medical safety, and environmental health [1–5]. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), a Gram-positive bacterium, is a highly concerning foodborne pathogen associated with various illnesses such as abscesses, wound infections, bacteremia, endocarditis, and other illnesses [6–9]. Concurrently, S. aureus is exceptionally infectious and resilient, capable of surviving temperatures ranging from 7 °C to 48.5 °C and at pH levels between 4.2 and 9.3 [6]. Furthermore, its early-stage contamination is challenging to detect, leading to common health and safety incidents. Therefore, accurate and sensitive detection of S. aureus is essential to prevent safety issues effectively. Current S. aureus detection methods include bacterial culture techniques, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays, and polymerase chain reactions [10–12]. Although these methods are still in use, they suffer from drawbacks, such as low sensitivity, time-consuming procedures, and complexity, hindering efficient S. aureus determination. Therefore, there is a pressing need for a simple, highly sensitive, accurate, rapid, and cost-effective method for S. aureus detection.

The electrochemical aptasensors are highly efficient detection technology with notable advantages, including high sensitivity, ease of miniaturization, and straightforward operational characteristics of electrochemical sensors [13–16]. Moreover, its application in S. aureus detection is increasingly valued due to the specificity afforded by aptamer recognition. In the aptasensor, probe DNA serves as the recognition element, facilitating reactions at the sensing interface of the working electrode and effectively converting biological changes into electrochemical signals using an electrochemical workstation. It is well recognized that the sensor-interface material in electrochemical aptasensors plays a pivotal role in shaping the sensing performance of these aptasensors [17,18]. Currently, several materials have been applied to achieve efficient S. aureus determination in electrochemical aptasensors, such as zeolite imidazolate framework-8/gold nanoparticles [19], Mesoporous polydopamine/MnO2 [20], gold nanoparticles/nitrogen-doped carbon nano-onions [21], and so on. Although these materials have made significant contributions, they often rely on single signal changes, making it challenging to eliminate electrochemical signals from inherent background interference in samples. To enhance the accuracy and overcome background interference, ratiometric electrochemical aptasensors with dual electrical signal outputs have been developed. Compared to single-signal output, ratiometric electrochemical aptasensors feature a built-in calibration system, which effectively mitigates system background interference, thereby improving aptasensor accuracy. The ratiometric electrochemical aptasensors typically employ two electroactive probes with straightforward signal resolutions. When the target binds to the electrode surface, the signal of one probe intensifies, while that of the other weakens [22,23]. By analyzing the ratio of the rising and falling signals, precise S. aureus measurements can be achieved. However, S. aureus detection using ratiometric electrochemical aptasensors is still in the early stages of research. The development of high-performance ratiometric electrochemical aptasensors is promising for advancing S. aureus detection methods.

Nowadays, nanomaterials, owing to their distinctive physical, chemical, and biological properties, find extensive applications in energy, environmental, biosensor, and various other fields [24–30]. Metal-organic framework (MOF), serving as hybrid porous nanomaterials, offer high porosity, large specific surface areas, and ease of functionalization, making them versatile for applications such as gas adsorption, gas separation, catalysis, energy storage, and biosensing [31–35]. Moreover, certain electroactive MOFs, such as Cu-, Co-, and Fe-based MOFs, can function as signal labels for electrochemical biosensors, rendering them promising candidates for constructing electrochemical aptasensors [36–38]. Despite their potential, most MOFs are limited in their use as sensing interface materials for electrochemical sensors because of their low electrical conductivity and instability in acidic or aqueous solvents [39,40]. Notably, recent studies have revealed the suitability of [Cu2.5(benzotriazole-5-COO)1.5(benzotriazole-5-COOH)0.5(μ3-OH)-(H2O)]·3 H2O (Cu-MOF), which is a three-dimensional honeycomb porous network prepared using Cu(II) as the metal center and 5-carboxybenzotriazole (H2btca) as the ligand. The Cu-MOF exhibits exceptional electrical activity and stability [41]. Its copper atom connections form ribbon chains along the b-axis direction, facilitating electron transport. Additionally, a honeycomb-shaped one-dimensional channel along the c-axis direction enables electrolyte storage. Furthermore, the Cu-MOF can serve as a direct response signal for electrochemical aptasensors. Therefore, Cu-MOF is a promising sensing interface material that has the potential to contribute to the development of innovative electrochemical aptasensors.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a prominent member of carbon nanomaterials, offer distinct advantages including optical stability, easy functionalization, good conductivity, high specific surface area, and straightforward synthesis [42–44]. These qualities make them promising candidates for applications in catalysis, biological imaging, and fluorescence analysis [45–51]. Certainly, GQDs are also widely used in the field of electrochemical sensors. For example, GQDs-based materials have been employed in the development of electrochemical sensors for dopamine [46,52], glutathione [53], cephalexin [54], etc. This underlines the potential of GQDs as exceptional electrochemical sensing interface materials, which holds promise for the development of high-performance electrochemical aptasensors for the efficient detection of S. aureus.

In this study, a sensitive, accurate, and stable ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of S. aureus based on GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposite has been developed. The GQDs were synthesized from graphene as a precursor under ultrasound. Then the GQDs were combined with Cu-MOF under ultrasound to prepare GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposite ( Scheme 1 A). Subsequently, a ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor was developed (Scheme 1 B). The GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposite was used to provide the output reference signal (I
Cu-MOF ), while the probe DNA-ferrocene (I
S2-Fc ) was used to produce a response signal. In the presence of S. aureus, S2-Fc reacted with bacteria and detached from the sensing surface, resulting in a gradual weakening of I
S2-FC and an increase in I
Cu-MOF . This approach enabled the construction of a ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor for S. aureus detection, demonstrating high sensitivity (0.97 CFU·mL−1) and specificity. Furthermore, the aptasensor successfully detected S. aureus in tap water, milk, Lonicera japonica, urine, and Zhangjiang River, highlighting its broad applicability. Additionally, this ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor design can be adapted for the detection of other foodborne pathogens, such as Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7), Bacillus cereus (B. cereus), Yersinia enterocolitica (Y. enterocolitica), and Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes), which indicate that the design scheme of the electrochemical aptasensor has good universality, providing a new approach for the preparation of new electrochemical aptasensors.
Please see the materials and instrumentations in the supplementary materials. The DNA sequences in this studies were shown in Table 1, which were purchased from Shenggong Bioengineering Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cu-MOF, GQDs and GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Cu-MOF was synthesized by previous reports with some modifications [41,63]: 0.6 mM of Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O, 0.4 mM of benzotriazole-5-carboxylic acid and 50.0 μL of 30 % HCl were dispersed in 4.0 mL H2O and 8.0 mL acetonitrile. After 5 min of ultrasonication, the mixture was placed in a 15.0 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, heated to 140.0 °C for 24 h, and allowed to cool slowly to room temperature. This process yielded blue Cu-MOF crystals that were washed and dried for storage.

Preparation of GQDs: Firstly, graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized by a modified Hummers method [64]. Subsequently, 20.0 mg of GO was combined with 15.0 mL of 98 % H2SO4 and 15.0 mL of 98 % HNO3, and the mixture was stirred for dispersion. Following this, the solution was ultrasonicated for 12 h and subsequently filtered. Afterward, the filtrate was slowly dialyzed in water for 24 h and adjusted to a final volume of 100.0 mL. Finally, the prepared GQDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.
The GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposite synthesis involved mixing 1.0 mg of Cu-MOF with 1.0 mL of GQDs’ solution and subjecting it to ultrasonication for 2 h to obtain the GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposite.

Firstly, 5.0 μL of GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposites were modified onto the surface of a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) and dried, creating GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE. Second, the GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE was immersed in a mixture of 1 mL of 0.1 mM PBS, 10.0 mM 1-ethyl-3-(3’-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), and 10.0 mM N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) for 3 h to activate the electrode. It was then rinsed with TE (10.0 mM Tris-HCl/1.0 mM ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid) and placed in 1.0 mL of 0.1 μM S1 for 3 h. Subsequently, the electrode was washed with TE buffer and water to prepare the S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE. Third, S2-Fc, which is specific for S. aureus recognition, was hybridized with S1 for 1 h, creating S2-Fc/S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE. In this step, I
S2-Fc in the system increased, whereas I
Cu-MOF decreased. Fourth, in the presence of S. aureus, S2-Fc is bound to the bacteria, causing it to dissociate from S1 and release S2-Fc from S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE. This resulted in the gradual weakening of I
S2-Fc and a gradual increase in I
Cu-MOF , successfully constructing a ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor for S. aureus detection.
Please see the supplementary materials .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of the ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, a sensitive, accurate, and stable ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of S. aureus was designed using GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposite. The GQDs served a dual purpose by enhancing the electrochemical performance and stability of the Cu-MOF and facilitating the immobilization of probe DNA (S1). Additionally, Cu-MOF exhibited electrical activity, effectively enhancing the sensor's performance and serving as the signal label of the electrochemical aptasensor. Therefore, using the nanocomposite as the interface material of the electrochemical aptasensor can enhance its performance. The working principle of the ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor is illustrated in Scheme 1 B. First, the GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposite was modified onto the SPCE surface by introducing I
Cu-MOF into the system. Second, S1 was fixed on the electrode surface using EDC/NHS, with non-specific sites blocked by BSA. The presence of S1 impeded electron transmission, resulting in a reduction in the I
Cu-MOF . Third, the S2-Fc specific for S. aureus recognition was hybridized with S1, resulting in a gradual enhancement of I
S2-Fc within the system, while I
Cu-MOF was concurrently attenuated. Finally, in the presence of S. aureus, S2-Fc reacted with the bacteria and detached from the electrode surface, causing I
S2-Fc to gradually decrease and I
Cu-MOF to increase, enabling the construction of a ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of S. aureus.

The morphology of the GQDs was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 A, the particle size distribution of the GQDs exhibited relative uniformity, with sizes mainly ranging from 1–5 nm, and a concentration in the 2–3 nm range (Fig. S1). In Fig. 1 B, a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the GQDs revealed a well-defined crystal lattice with a lattice spacing of 0.210 nm, corresponding to the characteristic (002) carbon crystal plane. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, shown in Fig. 1 C, identified the XRD peak at 25.2°, confirming the (002) crystal plane of the GQDs[65]. The surface chemical composition of the GQDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 1 D). The C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of GQDs are shown in Fig. 1 E and Fig. S2, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 E) exhibited four major peaks with peak positions at 283.9 eV, 284.7 eV, 286.0 eV, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to C-C, CC, C-OH/C-O-C, and CO bonds, respectively [66]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2 ) displayed peak positions at 531.7 eV and 533.0 eV, respectively, corresponding to the CO and C-O bonds, respectively [66]. These results align with those of prior studies, confirming the successful synthesis of the GQDs in this study.

TEM images were used to investigate the morphology and particle size of the material. Fig. 2 A and Fig. S3A showed that the particle size of Cu-MOF was relatively uniform, and the results of the particle size analyzer showed that its particle size is 497 nm (Fig. S3B). What’s more, Fig. 2 B exhibited the smooth edges of Cu-MOF. Subsequently, TEM analysis was performed to characterize the morphology of the GQDs/Cu-MOF composites. Fig. S3C and Fig. 2 C depicted that the particle size of GQDs/Cu-MOF is very uniform, with a particle size of approximately 505 nm (Fig. S3B). Fig. 2 D shows the successful loading of the GQDs onto the Cu-MOF. Further insight into the uniform distribution of C, N, O, and Cu elements across the GQDs/Cu-MOF composites was provided through TEM and elemental mapping images (Fig. 2 E). Both the Cu-MOF and GQDs/Cu-MOF composites underwent XRD characterization. In Fig. 2 F, the observed diffraction peaks closely matched the simulation results, confirming the high crystallinity of the Cu-MOF [41,63]. In addition, the XRD peak positions of the GQDs/Cu-MOF composites corresponded to those of the original Cu-MOF, indicating that the framework structure of Cu-MOF remained unaltered following interaction with GQDs, underscoring the stability of Cu-MOF.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy investigations led to the identification of functional groups. As shown in Fig. S4, the peaks at 1085 cm−1, 1377 cm−1, 1643 cm−1, and 3429 cm−1, which were related to the stretching vibrations of C-O, C-H, -COO-, and -OH groups, respectively [67,68]. The Cu-MOF also showed several typical absorption bands, the peaks at 1399 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N and CC groups, respectively. The symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes of the coordinated -COO- groups, were responsible for the strong bands at 1376 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 in turn. The bands at 486 cm−1 and 730 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of Cu-O in Cu-MOF [69]. In addition, the peak at 3445 cm−1 is characteristic of O-H bonds. The infrared spectrum of GQDs/Cu-MOF were similar to that of Cu-MOF, but the peaks at 3445 cm−1 is significantly increased, which is due to the introduction of GODs leading to an increase in the -OH, resulting in an enhancement of this characteristic peak. The FTIR spectra also indirectly indicates that GQDs/Cu-MOF has been successfully prepared in this study.

XPS was employed to further investigate the GQDs/Cu-MOF composites. As illustrated in Fig. 2 G, the survey spectrum exhibited peaks corresponding to the elements C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Cu 2p within the GQDs/Cu-MOF composites. A detailed analysis of the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S5A) revealed four primary peaks at 284.0 eV, 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, and 287.8 eV. These peaks can be attributed to distinct functional groups, namely C-C, CC, C-OH/C-O-C, and CO, respectively. In the O 1 s spectrum, shown in Fig. S5B, three distinct peaks were observed at 530.4 eV, 531.3 eV, and 532.4 eV. These peaks signified the presence of different oxygen species, including metal oxides, CO, and C-OH/C-O-C functionalities within the GQDs/Cu-MOF composites. Fig. S5C showed that there was only one peak in the N 1 s spectrum at 100 ev, corresponding to the C-N bond. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2 H, the binding energies of Cu were at 931.9 eV, 934.2 eV and 951.6 eV, 954.1 eV, accompanied by four satellite peaks at binding energies of 940.6 eV, 943.0 eV, 960.7 eV, 964.7 eV. These observations indicated the concurrent existence of Cu+ and Cu2+ states within the GQDs/Cu-MOF composites, laying the foundation for subsequent applications in electrochemical aptasensor [70]. Furthermore, this study also analyzed the surface charges of Cu-MOF and GQDs/Cu-MOF (Fig. S6). The Zeta potential of Cu-MOF is −8.63 mV, while the Zeta potential of GQDs/Cu-MOF is −16.61 mV, indicating charge transfer between GQDs and Cu-MOF, resulting in the formation of GQDs/Cu-MOF composites. The above results indicate that GQDs/Cu-MOF composites had been successfully prepared in this study.

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), known for its enhanced sensitivity compared to cyclic voltammograms, was employed to assess the electrode performance in a 1.0 mM PBS solution in this study. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when using SPCE tested in a pH= 7.0, 1.0 mM PBS solution, the system showed almost no peak current signal. However, when Cu-MOF/SPCE was used to detect 1.0 mM PBS solution, an oxidation peak current signal for Cu-MOF emerged, with an intensity of 1.115 μA, indicating the electrical activity of the prepared Cu-MOF. Upon surface modification of the SPCE with the GQDs/Cu-MOF nanocomposite, the electrochemical properties were gradually enhanced to 3.301 μA. The I
Cu-MOF was 2.961 times that of the Cu-MOF/SPCE, indicating the ability of the GQDs to effectively boost the electrochemical performance of the system and enhance its sensitivity. Subsequently, the loading time of S1 on the GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE was investigated. As depicted in Fig. S7, the electrochemical performance of the system stabilized after a 4 h reaction time between the GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE and S1. When measuring the S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE in a PBS solution, the I
Cu-MOF decreased to 2.751 μA, indirectly confirming the successful preparation of S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE. The mechanism of immobilizing S1 onto the electrode involves coupling the -NH2 groups on S1 with the -COOH groups of the GQDs/Cu-MOF under the catalysis of EDC/NHS. The DNA molecules, while improving the electrochemical properties, hindered electron transfer on the electrode surface, weakening the electrochemical signal. To verify that this immobilization mode is formed through the interaction between the NH2 groups on S1 and the -COOH groups on the surface of GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE, rather than through adsorption, an experiment was conducted using an S1 sequence without NH2 groups (S4: 5’-(CH2)6-ATT ATT ATT ATT TTT CTG CCC CCC CCC CTG GCC GCA-3’). The results showed that after interacting with the GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE electrode, there was almost no change in the DPV and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) signal of the system (Fig. S8), indicating that S4 could not be immobilized on the electrode surface. This demonstrated that successful immobilization onto the surface of the electrode occurs only when the -NH2 groups on S1 interact with the -COOH groups on the surface of GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE.

Further investigation of the hybridization reaction between S2-Fc and S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE revealed that the formation of S2-Fc/S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE resulted in a further decrease in I
Cu-MOF to 0.251 μA, while, the I
S2-Fc reached 4.523 μA. Fig. S9 shows that I
Cu-MOF and I
S2-Fc stabilized after a 60 min reaction time. Thus, a 60 min reaction time for S2-Fc hybridization with S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE was selected as the optimal condition. To validate that S2 is modified onto the surface of S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE through hybridization with S1, rather than through adsorption, an experiment was conducted using a single-stranded DNA (S5: 3’-Fc-CCC TCT TTT TTT TTT TCA ATT ATA ATG AGT GAA GAT GGG G-5’) that is completely non-complementary to S1. After reacting S5 with the S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE electrode, the DPV and EIS results showed that its signal was almost indistinguishable from that of the bare S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE electrode (Fig. S10), suggesting that S5 could not interact with the S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE electrode. These findings demonstrate that S2 is fixed onto the surface of S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE through DNA hybridization with S1, rather than through adsorption. This study further validated the construction process of the adapter sensor through zeta potential. As shown in Fig. S11, the zeta potential of GQDs/Cu-MOF was −16.61 mV. When it interacts with S1, the zeta potential decreased to −9.65 mV, indicating the successful formation of S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF composite material between S1 and GQDs/Cu-MOF. Afterwards, it reacted with S2-Fc to form S2-Fc/S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF, and the zeta potential decreased to −2.36 mV. However, when the S2-Fc/S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF reacted with 5.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1 S. aureus, the zeta potential returned to −8.71 mV. The main reason is that S. aureus could react with S2-Fc in S2-Fc/S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF, causing it to detach from the surface of the composite material, leading to the recovery of the zeta potential. The above results indicated that the sensor design had certain feasibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the electrochemical aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The incubation time between the aptasensor and S. aureus played a crucial role in the performance of the sensor. To determine the optimal incubation time, the different reaction times (4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, and 40 min) of S2 Fc/S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE with S. aureus at concentrations of 5.0 × 100 CFU·mL−1 and 5.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1 were investigated. At the beginning of the reaction, the ratio of I
Cu-MOF to I
S2-Fc in the system is the smallest, indicating that the signal of I
Cu-MOF is the smallest and the I
S2-Fc is the largest. After the reaction began, due to the reaction between S2-Fc and S. aureus, the S2-Fc detachment from the electrode surface, then the I
Cu-MOF gradually increased in proportion to I
S2-Fc . As shown in Fig. S12A and Fig. S12B, when the reaction reached 20 min, 5.0 × 100 CFU·mL−1 and 5.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1 concentrations of S. aureus reacted completely with S2-Fc/S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE, respectively. The pH of the system is another critical factor that affects the electrochemical performance of the aptasensor. Fig. S13 demonstrates that the electrochemical aptasensor performed optimally at a pH of 7.0 for a concentration of 5.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1 of S. aureus. Hence, a pH of 7.0 was selected as the optimal pH for this system.

Subsequently, the linear relationship between the concentrations of S. aureus and I
Cu-MOF /I
S2-Fc was investigated across a wide concentration (range from 5.0 × 100 to 5.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1, Fig. 3 B). As shown in Fig. 3 C, a strong linear correlation was observed between the logarithm of the S. aureus concentration (lgC) and I
Cu-MOF /I
S2-Fc , represented by the equation: I
Cu-MOF
/I
S2-Fc = 0.1416 lgC
S. aureus - 0.03714 (R2 = 0.9933). As known, the detection limit (LD) is an important factor in the performance of electrochemical aptasensors, which is mainly calculated through LD = 3Sb/k (Sb: standard deviation, k: the slope of the detection equation). By calculation, the method exhibited an impressive LD of 0.97 CFU·mL−1, surpassing the sensitivity of the previously reported methods (Table S1). This heightened sensitivity can be primarily attributed to the exceptional electrochemical performance of the GQDs/CuMOF nanocomposite. The GQDs exhibited excellent conductivity, thereby enhancing the sensitivity of the electrochemical aptasensor. Moreover, their surfaces are rich in carboxyl groups, contributing to the enhanced stability of the sensing interface. Additionally, the intrinsic electrical activity of the Cu-MOF effectively boosts the overall performance of the electrochemical aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, and stability of the electrochemical aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity, reproducibility, and stability of an aptasensor are crucial for its practical application. In this study, we tested its selectivity against interference targets such as E. coli O157:H7, B. cereus, Y. enterocolitica, L. monocytogenes, Staphylococcal enterotoxin B (S. enterotoxin B), Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium), Salmonella enteritidis (S. enteritidis), and Legionella penumophila (L. penumophila) as interfering substances. Fig. S14 showed the system signal remained nearly unchanged when detecting the interfering substances with the concentration of 5.0 × 104 CFU·mL−1, whereas it exhibited a significant change when detecting 5.0 × 102 CFU·mL−1 of S. aureus. This underscores the high specificity of the electrochemical aptasensor for S. aureus detection. Furthermore, we found that the performance of the electrochemical aptasensor was minimally affected when adding 5.0 × 104 CFU·mL−1 of other interfering substances to 5.0 × 102 CFU·mL−1 of S. aureus (Fig. 3 D). The result demonstrates that the electrochemical aptasensor has excellent selectivity.

Subsequently, the reproducibility of this ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor was investigated. We measured the I
Cu-MOF /I
S2-Fc responses from ten electrodes under the same modification (intra-assay, Fig. S15) and from a single electrode under ten repeated scans (inter-assay, Fig. 3 E) for 5.0 × 102 CFU·mL−1 of S. aureus. The relative standard deviation (RSD), which indicates the precision of the electrochemical aptasensor, was calculated using the formula:
RSD % =
∑
i = 1
n
(
x
i
−
x
̅
)
n − 1
x
̅
× 100 %
The calculated RSD for intra-assay was 2.33 %, and the RSD for inter-assay was 1.98 %. These results demonstrated the high accuracy and repeatability of the electrochemical aptasensor.

Moreover, the stability of the working electrode is a crucial factor influencing the electrochemical aptasensor performance. The S2-Fc/S1/GQDs/Cu-MOF/SPCE were stored at 4 °C and then tested at 5.0 × 102 CFU·mL−1 every day. As shown in Fig. 3 F, after 30 days, the I
Cu-MOF and I
S2-Fc decreased by only 2.11 % and 1.32 %, respectively, indicating the excellent stability of the electrochemical aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S. aureus in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the electrochemical aptasensor for detecting S. aureus was investigated using the standard addition method in various real samples, including tap water, milk, Lonicera japonica, urine, and Zhangjiang River. As detailed in Table 2, the recoveries obtained through electrochemical detection fell within the range of 97.30–106.80 %. The electrochemical aptasensor exhibited acceptable RSDs, ranging from 0.97 % to 2.02 %. Furthermore, the above results were in agreement with the ELISA. These findings confirm the high accuracy and reliability of the electrochemical aptasensor for S. aureus determination in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Universal verification of the sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the universality of the designed electrochemical aptasensor method, the design scheme of the sensor was used to construct various electrochemical aptasensors for the detection of different foodborne pathogens. Table 1 shows the DNA sequences of different targets and DNA probes. As shown in Fig. 4 A, a novel electrochemical aptasensor designed using this sensing method can detect E. coli O157:H7 in a concentration range of 5.0 × 100 - 5.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1, with a detection limit of 1.01 CFU·mL−1 (Fig. 4 B). What’s more, the detection of B. cereus (Fig. 4 C), L. monocytogenes (Fig. 4 E), and Y. enterocolitica (Fig. 4 G) can be achieved by using this sensing method, with detection ranges of 5.0 × 100 - 5.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1 (Figs. 4 D), 5.0 × 100 - 5.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1 (Figs. 4 F), and 1.0 × 101 - 1.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1 (Fig. 4 H), and detection limits of 1.05 CFU·mL−1, 2.01 CFU·mL−1, and 1.02 CFU·mL−1, respectively. The above results indicate that the design scheme of the electrochemical aptasensor in this study has good universality, and this method is expected to provide some reference for the development and commercial application of new electrochemical aptasensors.

In summary, we developed a high-performance ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor using GQDs/Cu-MOF for S. aureus detection. This electrochemical aptasensor showed remarkable sensitivity (0.97 CFU·mL−1), excellent stability, specificity, and a broad linear range (5.0 × 100 - 5.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1). Moreover, it effectively detected S. aureus in the real samples. Our approach leveraged a programmable dynamic range and diverse structural aptamer affinities, endowing the electrochemical aptasensor with the capability to swiftly detect threshold levels. The strategy of this sensor is universal, and through the design scheme of the aptasensor, the detection of various foodborne pathogens can be achieved, such as E. coli O157:H7, B. cereus, Y. enterocolitica, and L. monocytogenes. It will provide valuable tools for early detection of food safety and early warning of foodborne diseases, as well as new ideas for the design of new electrochemical aptasensors.

A wide array of Staphylococcus aureus usually found in the environment, food, and water, which is a highly concerning foodborne pathogen associated with various illnesses. The sensitive detection of Staphylococcus aureus holds great practical importance for ensuring public health and food safety. In this study, a sensitivity ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor using graphene quantum dots/Cu-MOF nanocomposite was constructed for the real-time monitoring of ultra-trace Staphylococcus aureu in tap water, milk, Lonicera japonica, urine, and Zhangjiang River. Additionally, this aptasensor design can be adapted for monitoring of other foodborne pathogens, which indicates that the design scheme of the aptasensor has good universality.
